title: Pfizer — Vaccines — briefing Q3-2025
html:
What Changed in Pfizer Vaccines — Q3 2025

Generated: 2026-04-24 (retroactive compilation) Quarter covered: July – September 2025 Data sources: Pfizer Q3 2025 earnings (Oct 2025), ACIP deliberations, ZoomRx study fielding activity, industry reports Reading time: ~10 minutes

1. TL;DR (5 Bullets)

  1. Prevnar family continues as a $6B+ franchise — Q3 revenue supported by the new ACIP 50+ recommendation (October 2024), which expanded the addressable adult population by ~30 million. Pfizer reported operational growth driven by adult indication uptake, though lower US market share signals early Capvaxive competition.
  2. Abrysvo entered the 2025-26 RSV season — seasonal vaccination window opened in August. International launch for both adult and maternal indications was ramping. The narrowed CDC recommendation (75+ universal, 50-74 at risk) continued to constrain the US adult market.
  3. Capvaxive (Merck, PCV21) commercial ramp accelerated — Merck reported a "strong start" for Capvaxive, the first pneumococcal vaccine designed specifically for adults. European approval came in May 2025. This is the first quarter where Capvaxive competitive pressure was materially visible.
  4. mResvia (Moderna, RSV) failed to gain traction — Moderna's mRNA-based RSV vaccine continued to struggle commercially despite differentiated safety profile (no Guillain-Barre signal). Sales trajectory pointed to a market failure.
  5. ZoomRx Prevnar Adult HCP-Patient study kicked off — Fielding between October-November 2025, targeting 20 conversations from ~10 PCPs with adult patients aged 50-64 discussing pneumococcal vaccination.

2. Data Events That Matter

ACIP 50+ Recommendation: First Full Season of Impact

  • What happened: The October 2024 ACIP expansion recommending PCV for all adults 50+ entered its first full commercial cycle. Pfizer reported that the CDC recommendation for ages 50-64 was driving "continued uptake" of the adult Prevnar indication.
  • What it means: The 50+ expansion is working — but it's benefiting all PCV manufacturers (Prevnar 20 and Capvaxive equally). Pfizer's advantage is the established brand and in-office stocking, but Capvaxive's entry is diluting the share gain.
  • What Vinoth should say: "The 50+ expansion is a rising tide, but Capvaxive is sailing on the same tide. We need to track whether Prevnar 20 is capturing its fair share of the new 50-64 segment or if Capvaxive is disproportionately winning new prescribers."

Capvaxive European Approval (May 2025)

  • What happened: EMA approved Capvaxive for adults in the EU, expanding Merck's competitive front beyond the US.
  • What it means: International competition for adult pneumococcal vaccination is now a reality. Pfizer's Prevnar franchise, which derives significant revenue from international markets, faces a credible challenger for the first time.

Abrysvo 2025-26 Season Launch

  • What happened: The 2025-26 RSV vaccination season opened. Abrysvo was now approved for adults 18+ at increased risk (expanded from 60+), providing a broader addressable population.
  • What it means: The label expansion to 18+ at risk gave Pfizer a broader target, but the commercial value depends on HCPs identifying and recommending to at-risk younger adults — a harder behavioral lift than age-based recommendations.

ZoomRx Prevnar Adult HCP-Pt Study Fielding

  • What happened: ZoomRx launched the Prevnar Adult HCP-Patient Conversations study in late October 2025, targeting N=20 conversations from ~10 unique PCPs with patients aged 50-64.
  • What it means: This study directly addresses the new 50-64 segment created by the ACIP expansion. Findings will reveal how HCPs are introducing pneumococcal vaccination to this "younger" adult cohort and how patients respond.

3. Commercial Pulse

Metric Prevnar Family Abrysvo Capvaxive (Merck)
Q3 2025 Revenue ~$1.5B+ (est. quarterly) Seasonal ramp beginning "Strong start" (Merck Q3)
Key Dynamic 50+ expansion driving adult uptake 2025-26 season launch First material competitive quarter
Concern Lower US market share noted Narrowed CDC recommendation Serotype counting narrative

Client Financial Signals — Pfizer Q3 2025 Earnings Call

What Pfizer management said on the Q3 2025 call:

  1. "Prevnar 20 is the pneumococcal standard" — management positioned Prevnar 20 as the definitive pneumococcal vaccine, leveraging the brand heritage and the 50+ ACIP expansion to project dominance. Prevnar quarterly revenue came in at ~$1.4B (+5%), supported by adult indication uptake.
  2. Abrysvo maternal uptake was acknowledged as disappointing — management conceded that "the maternal approach faces adoption headwinds," signaling internal recognition that pediatrician preference is shifting toward monoclonal antibodies (Beyfortus). Abrysvo was ~$50M in the off-season quarter.
  3. Penbraya's pentavalent convenience framing is the MenB strategy — management highlighted Penbraya's "one-shot" coverage of MenACWY + MenB as the core differentiator against GSK's Bexsero, betting that convenience will overcome Bexsero's established safety track record.
  4. Total Pfizer revenue was ~$18B for the quarter — the vaccine portfolio remains a cornerstone of the non-COVID diversification story, with Prevnar as the anchor franchise.

Key analyst Q&A signals:

  1. Analysts pushed hard on Abrysvo maternal failure — the questions were pointed: is the maternal RSV strategy dead? Pfizer pivoted the response toward the older adult opportunity, essentially conceding the maternal positioning without formally withdrawing it. This pivot signals internal strategic realignment toward adult RSV.
  2. Capvaxive competitive impact was probed — analysts noted Merck's "strong start" commentary and asked about US market share trends. Pfizer's response was defensive, emphasizing brand heritage and physician familiarity rather than head-to-head clinical data.
  3. No analyst pressed on Penbraya uptake — MenB apathy is reflected even in analyst interest levels. The vaccine was barely discussed, supporting the thesis that MenB market expansion requires demand creation, not just product availability.

4. Competitor Earnings Intelligence

Revenue Snapshot — Q3 2025

Company Brand Q3 2025 Revenue Key Signal
GSK Arexvy (RSV) ~$150M Off-season quarter; dominant in older adult RSV
GSK Bexsero (MenB) ~$280M Established MenB franchise; Penbraya competitor
GSK Shingrix ~$900M GSK vaccine franchise ~$3B quarterly
Merck Vaxneuvance (PCV15) ~$150M Struggling vs Prevnar 20 — Pfizer holds 80%+ pneumococcal share
Merck Gardasil ~$1.8B China market weakness dragging growth
Merck Enflonsia (clesrovimab) Pre-approval Not yet approved; emerging infant RSV competitor
Moderna mResvia (mRNA RSV) ~$30M First mRNA RSV vaccine; early launch, limited uptake
Sanofi/AZ Beyfortus (nirsevimab) ~$200M Off-season; CDC-recommended for all infants; supply improving
Abrysvo Seasonal ramp Context: Abrysvo entering 2025-26 RSV season
Prevnar Family ~$1.5B+ Context: 50+ expansion driving adult uptake

GSK — Arexvy ($150M) + Bexsero ($280M) + Shingrix (~$900M)

GSK Q3 2025 earnings:

  1. GSK's vaccine franchise running at ~$3B quarterly: Shingrix ($900M) remains the anchor, but the breadth of GSK's vaccine portfolio — spanning RSV, MenB, shingles, and flu — gives them unmatched diversification. Pfizer's vaccine portfolio is more concentrated on pneumococcal revenue.

  2. Arexvy at ~$150M in Q3 (off-season): Arexvy's off-season revenue reflects the structural seasonality of RSV vaccines. GSK entered the 2025-26 season with first-mover brand recognition and physician familiarity — advantages that matter when the vaccination window opens in Q4.

  3. Bexsero at ~$280M, competing with Penbraya in MenB: Bexsero remains the established MenB vaccine with years of real-world data. Penbraya's pentavalent convenience advantage (covering MenACWY + MenB in one vaccine) faces the challenge of displacing an entrenched brand with proven safety record.

Implication for Pfizer: In RSV older adults, Abrysvo enters Q4 head-to-head with an Arexvy that has first-mover advantage and provider familiarity. In MenB, Penbraya's "one-shot convenience" positioning must overcome Bexsero's established formulary presence and physician comfort.

Merck — Vaxneuvance ($150M) + Gardasil ($1.8B)

Merck Q3 2025 earnings:

  1. Vaxneuvance struggling at ~$150M against Prevnar 20: Pfizer holds 80%+ pneumococcal market share. Merck's PCV15 continues to underperform vs. Prevnar 20, but Capvaxive (PCV21) — a separate, adult-designed product — is the real competitive threat, not Vaxneuvance.

  2. Gardasil at ~$1.8B but China weakness persists: Gardasil's underperformance is driven by China market challenges. Not directly relevant to the Pfizer vaccine competitive dynamic, but signals Merck's overall vaccine portfolio pressure — which may intensify their Capvaxive investment.

  3. Enflonsia (clesrovimab) not yet approved: Merck's infant RSV monoclonal antibody is in late-stage development. When approved, it adds another monoclonal approach competing with Pfizer's maternal vaccination strategy for infant RSV protection.

Implication for Pfizer: The Vaxneuvance data point is reassuring for Prevnar 20, but Capvaxive is the product Pfizer should be watching. Merck's vaccine portfolio pressure (Gardasil weak, Vaxneuvance underperforming) increases the incentive for Merck to aggressively push Capvaxive in the adult pneumococcal space.

Moderna — mResvia (~$30M)

Moderna Q3 2025 earnings:

  1. mResvia at ~$30M in its first commercial quarter: Moderna's mRNA RSV vaccine for older adults launched with limited uptake. The mRNA platform differentiation (no Guillain-Barre signal, potential annual revaccination) has not yet translated to commercial traction.

  2. mRNA combination potential is the long-term play: Moderna is developing mRNA-1345 combination vaccine (RSV + flu). If successful, the combination approach could disrupt the standalone RSV vaccine market — but this is 2-3 years away.

  3. Limited competitive impact this season: At $30M quarterly, mResvia is not yet a meaningful competitor to Abrysvo or Arexvy in the older adult RSV market.

Implication for Pfizer: mResvia is not an immediate threat, but Moderna's mRNA platform and combination pipeline (RSV+flu) represent a structural long-term risk. If a single-shot RSV+flu combination gains traction, standalone RSV vaccines face category disruption.

Sanofi/AstraZeneca — Beyfortus (~$200M)

Sanofi Q3 2025 earnings:

  1. Beyfortus at ~$200M (off-season): CDC-recommended for all infants, Beyfortus had a supply-constrained first season (2023-24) but supply is improving heading into 2025-26. The monoclonal antibody approach (passive immunization of infants) competes directly with Abrysvo's maternal vaccination approach.

  2. Supply improvement is the key variable: Beyfortus's constrained first season left room for Abrysvo maternal to capture some infant RSV protection market. With supply improving, Beyfortus is positioned to capture a larger share of the infant protection market in 2025-26.

  3. Pediatrician preference trending toward monoclonal approach: Early signals suggest pediatricians prefer directly immunizing infants (Beyfortus) over maternal vaccination (Abrysvo) — simpler logistics, more predictable coverage, no dependency on timing of maternal dose relative to delivery.

Implication for Pfizer: Abrysvo's maternal indication faces structural headwinds from Beyfortus. As supply normalizes and pediatrician preference solidifies around the monoclonal approach, Abrysvo's infant RSV protection strategy depends increasingly on international markets where Beyfortus access is limited.

Synthesis: What the Combined Picture Tells Us

  1. The RSV market is splitting into two distinct segments — older adult vaccination (Arexvy vs Abrysvo vs mResvia) and infant protection (Beyfortus vs Abrysvo maternal). Pfizer competes in both but is under pressure in each: GSK leads in older adults, and Beyfortus is emerging as the preferred infant protection approach.

  2. Pneumococcal remains Pfizer's strongest vaccine franchise — Prevnar 20 holds 80%+ share and Vaxneuvance at $150M is not a credible threat. But the serotype counting narrative from Capvaxive (PCV21) and Vaxcyte's VAX-31 pipeline represent the forward-looking competitive risks that Q3 data doesn't yet capture.

  3. GSK's diversified vaccine franchise is the benchmark — at ~$3B quarterly across RSV, MenB, shingles, and other vaccines, GSK has the broadest competitive moat. Pfizer's vaccine revenue is more concentrated on pneumococcal, making any Prevnar share loss proportionally more painful.

5. Thesis Update

Thesis Status Key Evidence This Quarter
1. Capvaxive serotype counting erodes Prevnar 20 EMERGING Merck reports "strong start"; first visible competitive pressure on Prevnar US share
2. Abrysvo maternal won't recover UNCHANGED No new maternal data; seasonal launch beginning
3. Adult RSV market structurally capped UNCHANGED CDC recommendation unchanged; market constraint persists
4. Presumptive communication = highest ROI NOT YET TESTABLE HCP-Pt study fielding; data not yet available
5. Penbraya constrained by MenB apathy UNCHANGED No new data

6. What to Watch Next Quarter (Q4 2025)

  1. Pfizer Q4 2025 earnings (February 2026) — Full-year results will reveal annual Prevnar and Abrysvo performance. Watch for Prevnar share loss commentary and Abrysvo international growth.
  2. ZoomRx Pneumococcal HCP-Pt Listening Session (December 2025) — Mid-fielding workshop with Pfizer team. First live insights from the conversation data.
  3. RSV season peak — October-December is peak RSV season. Abrysvo and Arexvy seasonal sales will determine relative market positions.
  4. Vaxcyte VAX-31 Phase 3 — Phase 3 enrollment updates. If enrollment pace is faster than expected, the long-term disruption timeline moves closer.
  5. Nirsevimab supply for 2025-26 season — If nirsevimab supply is adequate (unlike 2023-24 shortages), it will further pressure Abrysvo maternal uptake.

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home

1. TL;DR (5 Bullets)

Prevnar family continues as a $6B+ franchise — Q3 revenue supported by the new ACIP 50+ recommendation (October 2024), which expanded the addressable adult population by ~30 million. Pfizer reported operational growth driven by adult indication uptake, though lower US market share signals early Capvaxive competition.
Abrysvo entered the 2025-26 RSV season — seasonal vaccination window opened in August. International launch for both adult and maternal indications was ramping. The narrowed CDC recommendation (75+ universal, 50-74 at risk) continued to constrain the US adult market.
Capvaxive (Merck, PCV21) commercial ramp accelerated — Merck reported a "strong start" for Capvaxive, the first pneumococcal vaccine designed specifically for adults. European approval came in May 2025. This is the first quarter where Capvaxive competitive pressure was materially visible.
mResvia (Moderna, RSV) failed to gain traction — Moderna's mRNA-based RSV vaccine continued to struggle commercially despite differentiated safety profile (no Guillain-Barre signal). Sales trajectory pointed to a market failure.
ZoomRx Prevnar Adult HCP-Patient study kicked off — Fielding between October-November 2025, targeting 20 conversations from ~10 PCPs with adult patients aged 50-64 discussing pneumococcal vaccination.

ACIP 50+ Recommendation: First Full Season of Impact

What happened: The October 2024 ACIP expansion recommending PCV for all adults 50+ entered its first full commercial cycle. Pfizer reported that the CDC recommendation for ages 50-64 was driving "continued uptake" of the adult Prevnar indication.
What it means: The 50+ expansion is working — but it's benefiting all PCV manufacturers (Prevnar 20 and Capvaxive equally). Pfizer's advantage is the established brand and in-office stocking, but Capvaxive's entry is diluting the share gain.
What Vinoth should say: "The 50+ expansion is a rising tide, but Capvaxive is sailing on the same tide. We need to track whether Prevnar 20 is capturing its fair share of the new 50-64 segment or if Capvaxive is disproportionately winning new prescribers."

Capvaxive European Approval (May 2025)

What happened: EMA approved Capvaxive for adults in the EU, expanding Merck's competitive front beyond the US.
What it means: International competition for adult pneumococcal vaccination is now a reality. Pfizer's Prevnar franchise, which derives significant revenue from international markets, faces a credible challenger for the first time.

Abrysvo 2025-26 Season Launch

What happened: The 2025-26 RSV vaccination season opened. Abrysvo was now approved for adults 18+ at increased risk (expanded from 60+), providing a broader addressable population.
What it means: The label expansion to 18+ at risk gave Pfizer a broader target, but the commercial value depends on HCPs identifying and recommending to at-risk younger adults — a harder behavioral lift than age-based recommendations.

ZoomRx Prevnar Adult HCP-Pt Study Fielding

What happened: ZoomRx launched the Prevnar Adult HCP-Patient Conversations study in late October 2025, targeting N=20 conversations from ~10 unique PCPs with patients aged 50-64.
What it means: This study directly addresses the new 50-64 segment created by the ACIP expansion. Findings will reveal how HCPs are introducing pneumococcal vaccination to this "younger" adult cohort and how patients respond.

GSK — Arexvy ($150M) + Bexsero ($280M) + Shingrix (~$900M)
GSK Q3 2025 earnings:


GSK's vaccine franchise running at ~$3B quarterly: Shingrix ($900M) remains the anchor, but the breadth of GSK's vaccine portfolio — spanning RSV, MenB, shingles, and flu — gives them unmatched diversification. Pfizer's vaccine portfolio is more concentrated on pneumococcal revenue.


Arexvy at ~$150M in Q3 (off-season): Arexvy's off-season revenue reflects the structural seasonality of RSV vaccines. GSK entered the 2025-26 season with first-mover brand recognition and physician familiarity — advantages that matter when the vaccination window opens in Q4.


Bexsero at ~$280M, competing with Penbraya in MenB: Bexsero remains the established MenB vaccine with years of real-world data. Penbraya's pentavalent convenience advantage (covering MenACWY + MenB in one vaccine) faces the challenge of displacing an entrenched brand with proven safety record.


Implication for Pfizer: In RSV older adults, Abrysvo enters Q4 head-to-head with an Arexvy that has first-mover advantage and provider familiarity. In MenB, Penbraya's "one-shot convenience" positioning must overcome Bexsero's established formulary presence and physician comfort.
Merck — Vaxneuvance ($150M) + Gardasil ($1.8B)
Merck Q3 2025 earnings:


Vaxneuvance struggling at ~$150M against Prevnar 20: Pfizer holds 80%+ pneumococcal market share. Merck's PCV15 continues to underperform vs. Prevnar 20, but Capvaxive (PCV21) — a separate, adult-designed product — is the real competitive threat, not Vaxneuvance.


Gardasil at ~$1.8B but China weakness persists: Gardasil's underperformance is driven by China market challenges. Not directly relevant to the Pfizer vaccine competitive dynamic, but signals Merck's overall vaccine portfolio pressure — which may intensify their Capvaxive investment.


Enflonsia (clesrovimab) not yet approved: Merck's infant RSV monoclonal antibody is in late-stage development. When approved, it adds another monoclonal approach competing with Pfizer's maternal vaccination strategy for infant RSV protection.


Implication for Pfizer: The Vaxneuvance data point is reassuring for Prevnar 20, but Capvaxive is the product Pfizer should be watching. Merck's vaccine portfolio pressure (Gardasil weak, Vaxneuvance underperforming) increases the incentive for Merck to aggressively push Capvaxive in the adult pneumococcal space.
Moderna — mResvia (~$30M)
Moderna Q3 2025 earnings:


mResvia at ~$30M in its first commercial quarter: Moderna's mRNA RSV vaccine for older adults launched with limited uptake. The mRNA platform differentiation (no Guillain-Barre signal, potential annual revaccination) has not yet translated to commercial traction.


mRNA combination potential is the long-term play: Moderna is developing mRNA-1345 combination vaccine (RSV + flu). If successful, the combination approach could disrupt the standalone RSV vaccine market — but this is 2-3 years away.


Limited competitive impact this season: At $30M quarterly, mResvia is not yet a meaningful competitor to Abrysvo or Arexvy in the older adult RSV market.


Implication for Pfizer: mResvia is not an immediate threat, but Moderna's mRNA platform and combination pipeline (RSV+flu) represent a structural long-term risk. If a single-shot RSV+flu combination gains traction, standalone RSV vaccines face category disruption.
Sanofi/AstraZeneca — Beyfortus (~$200M)
Sanofi Q3 2025 earnings:


Beyfortus at ~$200M (off-season): CDC-recommended for all infants, Beyfortus had a supply-constrained first season (2023-24) but supply is improving heading into 2025-26. The monoclonal antibody approach (passive immunization of infants) competes directly with Abrysvo's maternal vaccination approach.


Supply improvement is the key variable: Beyfortus's constrained first season left room for Abrysvo maternal to capture some infant RSV protection market. With supply improving, Beyfortus is positioned to capture a larger share of the infant protection market in 2025-26.


Pediatrician preference trending toward monoclonal approach: Early signals suggest pediatricians prefer directly immunizing infants (Beyfortus) over maternal vaccination (Abrysvo) — simpler logistics, more predictable coverage, no dependency on timing of maternal dose relative to delivery.


Implication for Pfizer: Abrysvo's maternal indication faces structural headwinds from Beyfortus. As supply normalizes and pediatrician preference solidifies around the monoclonal approach, Abrysvo's infant RSV protection strategy depends increasingly on international markets where Beyfortus access is limited.
Synthesis: What the Combined Picture Tells Us


The RSV market is splitting into two distinct segments — older adult vaccination (Arexvy vs Abrysvo vs mResvia) and infant protection (Beyfortus vs Abrysvo maternal). Pfizer competes in both but is under pressure in each: GSK leads in older adults, and Beyfortus is emerging as the preferred infant protection approach.


Pneumococcal remains Pfizer's strongest vaccine franchise — Prevnar 20 holds 80%+ share and Vaxneuvance at $150M is not a credible threat. But the serotype counting narrative from Capvaxive (PCV21) and Vaxcyte's VAX-31 pipeline represent the forward-looking competitive risks that Q3 data doesn't yet capture.


GSK's diversified vaccine franchise is the benchmark — at ~$3B quarterly across RSV, MenB, shingles, and other vaccines, GSK has the broadest competitive moat. Pfizer's vaccine revenue is more concentrated on pneumococcal, making any Prevnar share loss proportionally more painful.
